meta.title: SF-SPAA-3: Storage, Compute Optimization and Cost Management

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to view all existing users in the account along with their default roles and the date they last logged in. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS;
SELECT * FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.USERS;
SHOW USERS;
DESCRIBE ALL USERS;

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. SHOW USERS displays all users with their properties including login_name, display_name, default_role, default_warehouse, last_success_login, and other metadata.

**A** is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS provides queryable historical data with up to a 2-hour latency — useful for reporting but not the direct DDL command for live user inspection.

**B** is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA does not have a USERS view; user metadata is not exposed through INFORMATION_SCHEMA.

**D** is incorrect. DESCRIBE ALL USERS is not valid Snowflake syntax.

[SHOW USERS](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/show-users)

questions.stem: A company's security policy requires that all Snowflake passwords be at least 16 characters, contain at least 2 uppercase letters, 2 lowercase letters, 2 numbers, and 2 special characters, and not match any of the last 5 passwords. Which CREATE PASSWORD POLICY statement implements these requirements?

options.text: CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy PASSWORD_MIN_LENGTH = 16 PASSWORD_MIN_UPPER_CASE_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_MIN_LOWER_CASE_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_MIN_NUMERIC_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_MIN_SPECIAL_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_HISTORY = 5;
CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy MIN_LENGTH = 16 COMPLEXITY = (UPPER=2, LOWER=2, NUMERIC=2, SPECIAL=2) HISTORY = 5;
CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy WITH (MIN_LENGTH = 16, MIN_UPPERCASE = 2, MIN_LOWERCASE = 2, MIN_DIGITS = 2, MIN_SPECIAL = 2, MAX_REUSE = 5);
CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy ENFORCE MIN_LENGTH 16 COMPLEXITY UPPER 2 LOWER 2 NUMERIC 2 SPECIAL 2 NO_REUSE 5;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Snowflake PASSWORD POLICY parameters use fully prefixed names: PASSWORD_MIN_LENGTH, PASSWORD_MIN_UPPER_CASE_CHARS, PASSWORD_MIN_LOWER_CASE_CHARS, PASSWORD_MIN_NUMERIC_CHARS, PASSWORD_MIN_SPECIAL_CHARS, and PASSWORD_HISTORY.

**B** is incorrect. MIN_LENGTH and COMPLEXITY are not valid Snowflake password policy parameter names.

**C** is incorrect. The WITH (...) clause and parameter names MIN_UPPERCASE, MIN_DIGITS, MAX_REUSE do not match Snowflake's actual syntax.

**D** is incorrect. ENFORCE and NO_REUSE are not valid Snowflake password policy keywords.

[CREATE PASSWORD POLICY](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/create-password-policy)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is auditing the account's role hierarchy and discovers a custom role FINANCE_ADMIN that was created but never added to the standard hierarchy. Select TWO consequences of this configuration gap. (Select TWO)

options.text: Objects created by roles under FINANCE_ADMIN are not visible to SYSADMIN through normal role hierarchy inheritance
Users cannot be granted the FINANCE_ADMIN role until it is added to the hierarchy
ACCOUNTADMIN cannot view or manage objects owned by FINANCE_ADMIN
Only ACCOUNTADMIN can manage users and objects associated with FINANCE_ADMIN because it is outside the normal hierarchy

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. When a custom role is not granted to SYSADMIN, objects owned by that role are not accessible to SYSADMIN through hierarchy inheritance — SYSADMIN cannot manage or drop those objects.

**B** is incorrect. Users can be granted any role regardless of where it sits in the hierarchy; a role does not need to be in the SYSADMIN chain to be assignable.

**C** is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN inherits all roles including orphaned ones and always retains full visibility and management capability.

**D** is correct. ACCOUNTADMIN is the only role that can manage users and objects associated with FINANCE_ADMIN because it is outside the SYSADMIN-managed hierarchy.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A Snowflake account is on Enterprise edition. An administrator sets the account-level MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 14. A table owner sets DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 90 on a specific table. What is the effective Time Travel retention for that table?

options.text: 14 days, because MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS caps the retention at 14 days
90 days, because the table-level setting overrides the account-level MAX setting
90 days, because MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS controls Fail-Safe extension, not Time Travel retention
7 days, which is the Enterprise edition maximum for Time Travel

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS controls how long Snowflake can extend data retention beyond the set Time Travel period when needed by Streams consuming the table — it does not cap DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS. The table's retention of 90 days is the effective Time Travel retention. On Enterprise edition, the maximum Time Travel retention is 90 days.

**A** is incorrect. MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS is not a cap on DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS — it serves a different purpose related to stream staleness.

**B** is incorrect in its reasoning. The table-level setting does override the account-level setting, but MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS is not a cap on Time Travel retention at all.

**D** is incorrect. Enterprise edition supports up to 90 days of Time Travel, not 7 days (7 days is the Standard edition maximum).

[Understanding & Using Time Travel](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel)

questions.stem: A company uses Snowflake at the Organization level with multiple accounts (PROD, DEV, STAGING). An administrator needs to move a database from the DEV account to the PROD account. Which approach is supported natively in Snowflake?

options.text: Use ALTER DATABASE dev_db TRANSFER TO ACCOUNT prod_account;
Export all tables to an external stage, then import into the PROD account
Use the ORGADMIN role to run MOVE DATABASE dev_db TO ACCOUNT prod_account;
Use database replication (CREATE DATABASE prod_db AS REPLICA OF dev_account.dev_db) to create a replica in PROD, then promote it

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Snowflake's database replication feature allows creating a replica of a database in another account within the same organization. The workflow is: enable replication on the source, create a replicated database in the target account, refresh the replica, and optionally promote it to a primary.

**A** is incorrect. ALTER DATABASE TRANSFER is not a valid Snowflake command.

**C** is incorrect. MOVE DATABASE is not a valid Snowflake command — ORGADMIN does not have such a syntax.

**B** is incorrect. External stage export/import works but is a manual data migration, not a native first-class database move feature.

[Introduction to Replication and Failover](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/account-replication-intro)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to configure the account so that all users are required to authenticate using SSO (SAML 2.0) through their corporate identity provider, with no password-based login allowed. Which steps are required?

options.text: Configure a SAML integration, set the default_login to SSO, and run ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOW_PASSWORD_AUTHENTICATION = FALSE
Run CREATE SAML PROVIDER, then run ALTER ALL USERS SET AUTHENTICATION = SSO_ONLY
Create a SECURITY INTEGRATION of type SAML2, configure the IdP, and optionally disable native password auth via a LOGIN POLICY with ALLOW_PASSWORD_AUTHENTICATION = FALSE
Enable SSO in the Snowflake UI settings and set REQUIRE_SSO = TRUE in a session policy

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. SSO via SAML2 requires creating a SECURITY INTEGRATION (CREATE SECURITY INTEGRATION ... TYPE = SAML2) configured with the identity provider's metadata. To prevent password-based logins, a LOGIN POLICY with ALLOW_PASSWORD_AUTHENTICATION = FALSE is applied at the account or user level.

**A** is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOW_PASSWORD_AUTHENTICATION is not a valid Snowflake account parameter — the correct mechanism is a LOGIN POLICY object.

**B** is incorrect. CREATE SAML PROVIDER and ALTER ALL USERS SET AUTHENTICATION = SSO_ONLY are not valid Snowflake syntax.

**D** is incorrect. REQUIRE_SSO is not a valid session policy attribute — session policies control timeout and other session behaviors, not authentication method enforcement.

[Federated Authentication and SSO](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-fed-auth-overview)

questions.stem: An administrator is setting up Snowflake for a new team and needs to create a proper role and object structure. The team needs a custom role DATASCIENCE_ROLE that will own a database DS_DB. Select TWO commands that correctly establish the intended ownership structure. (Select TWO)

options.text: USE ROLE SYSADMIN; CREATE ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE; GRANT ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE TO ROLE SYSADMIN;
USE ROLE SYSADMIN; CREATE DATABASE DS_DB; GRANT OWNERSHIP ON DATABASE DS_DB TO ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE;
USE ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE; CREATE DATABASE DS_DB; (This creates the database owned by DATASCIENCE_ROLE)
USE ROLE SECURITYADMIN; CREATE ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE; GRANT ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE TO ROLE SYSADMIN;

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. When a database is created while using DATASCIENCE_ROLE (USE ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE; CREATE DATABASE DS_DB), the creating role automatically becomes the owner.

**D** is correct. Creating the role under SECURITYADMIN and granting it to SYSADMIN follows Snowflake best practice: SECURITYADMIN manages roles and users; granting to SYSADMIN keeps the custom role within the managed hierarchy.

**A** is incorrect. Using SYSADMIN to create roles is technically possible but not best practice — SECURITYADMIN is the appropriate role for role creation.

**B** is incorrect. SYSADMIN creating the database and then transferring ownership with GRANT OWNERSHIP is a valid workflow but less direct than option C, and SYSADMIN briefly owns the object.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A company needs to restrict the maximum duration that any individual SQL statement can run, regardless of the warehouse. Which account parameter controls this?

options.text: LOCK_TIMEOUT
QUERY_TAG
STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS
SESSION_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS sets the maximum seconds a SQL statement can run before it is automatically cancelled. It can be set at the account, warehouse, or session level.

**A** is incorrect. LOCK_TIMEOUT controls how long a query waits to acquire a lock — it does not limit total execution time.

**B** is incorrect. QUERY_TAG is a metadata label for tracking queries in query history — it has no effect on execution time.

**D** is incorrect. SESSION_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS is not a standard Snowflake account parameter. Idle session timeout is managed through session policies (CLIENT_SESSION_KEEP_ALIVE and related properties).

[Parameters](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/parameters)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to set up a resource monitor that sends a notification email when 80% of the monthly quota is consumed, suspends the warehouse when 100% is reached, but does NOT want to create a new trigger for every 10% above 100%. Which trigger configuration is needed?

options.text: TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND ON 110 PERCENT DO SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY
TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO ALERT ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND
TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND
TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 110 PERCENT DO SUSPEND

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. The requirement is exactly two triggers: NOTIFY at 80% and SUSPEND at 100%. SUSPEND allows running queries to finish before suspending the warehouse. No additional triggers are needed above 100%.

**A** is incorrect. Adding SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY at 110% introduces an extra trigger that the requirement explicitly excludes.

**B** is incorrect. ALERT is not a valid resource monitor trigger action — the valid actions are NOTIFY, SUSPEND, and SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY.

**D** is incorrect. Having NOTIFY at 100% instead of SUSPEND delays the suspension action until 110%, which contradicts the requirement to suspend at exactly 100%.

[Resource Monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: A company wants to analyze which virtual warehouses have consumed the most credits over the past 7 days, grouped by hour. Which query provides this information?

options.text: SELECT WAREHOUSE_NAME, DATE_TRUNC('HOUR', START_TIME) AS HOUR, SUM(CREDITS_USED) FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY WHERE START_TIME >= DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()) GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC;
SELECT SERVICE_TYPE, USAGE_DATE, SUM(CREDITS_USED) FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY WHERE USAGE_DATE >= DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_DATE()) GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC;
SELECT WAREHOUSE_NAME, CREDITS_USED FROM SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY WHERE START_TIME >= DATEADD('hour', -168, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()) ORDER BY CREDITS_USED DESC;
SELECT * FROM TABLE(SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY(DATE_RANGE_START => DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_DATE())));

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY provides per-warehouse, per-hour credit consumption. Grouping by warehouse and DATE_TRUNC to the hour over the past 7 days produces exactly the hourly breakdown requested.

**B** is incorrect. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides daily granularity and does not break down by individual warehouse — it shows service-type totals.

**C** is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY is a table-valued function form with 14-day retention, but the syntax shown is missing required parameters and the needed aggregation.

**D** is incorrect. The INFORMATION_SCHEMA table function form also works for 14-day lookbacks but does not aggregate by hour as required.

[WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY View](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/warehouse-metering-history)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to alert the finance team when daily credit consumption across all warehouses exceeds 500 credits. Which TWO approaches can provide this capability? (Select TWO)

options.text: Create a resource monitor at the account level with CREDIT_QUOTA = 500 and FREQUENCY = DAILY, trigger action NOTIFY at 100%
Create a Snowflake Alert that queries METERING_DAILY_HISTORY and sends a notification when the daily total exceeds 500
Set ALTER ACCOUNT SET MAX_DAILY_CREDITS = 500 to automatically alert and stop compute when the limit is reached
Create a Snowflake Task that runs daily, queries WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY, and calls SYSTEM$SEND_EMAIL if the threshold is exceeded

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. A daily account-level resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 500 and FREQUENCY = DAILY is a built-in Snowflake mechanism specifically for daily credit threshold notifications.

**B** is correct. Snowflake Alerts allow scheduling a condition check query against ACCOUNT_USAGE views (like METERING_DAILY_HISTORY) and triggering notifications when a threshold is exceeded — providing flexible, custom threshold monitoring.

**C** is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT SET MAX_DAILY_CREDITS is not a valid Snowflake account parameter.

**D** is incorrect. Snowflake Tasks combined with SYSTEM$SEND_EMAIL could technically work as a custom solution but requires significantly more setup; Alerts are the more direct built-in feature.

[Resource Monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: An administrator notices that Snowpipe is generating unexpectedly high serverless credit charges. Resource monitors assigned to the production warehouses show healthy consumption. Why are the Snowpipe charges not captured in the warehouse-level resource monitors?

options.text: Snowpipe credits are counted toward whichever warehouse processed the most data that day
Snowpipe requires a dedicated warehouse; the administrator needs to create a SNOWPIPE warehouse and assign the monitor to it
Snowpipe credits appear in resource monitors only after a 24-hour delay
Snowpipe uses serverless compute that is separate from virtual warehouses; resource monitors only track virtual warehouse credits, not serverless credits

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Snowpipe is a serverless feature — it uses Snowflake-managed compute, not customer virtual warehouses. Resource monitors only track credits consumed by virtual warehouses. Serverless credits (Snowpipe, automatic clustering, materialized view maintenance, search optimization) are billed separately and are not captured by resource monitors.

**A** is incorrect. Snowpipe credits are not attributed to any virtual warehouse — they have their own billing pathway entirely separate from warehouse compute.

**C** is incorrect. There is no 24-hour delay for Snowpipe charges to appear in resource monitors — they simply never appear because resource monitors do not track serverless compute at all.

**B** is incorrect. Snowpipe does not require or use a customer-managed warehouse — it is a fully serverless service.

[Resource Monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: A resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 1000 and FREQUENCY = MONTHLY is assigned to both the ANALYTICS_WH and REPORTING_WH. After 15 days, ANALYTICS_WH has consumed 700 credits and REPORTING_WH has consumed 200 credits. The NOTIFY trigger is set at 80%. What is the current status?

options.text: No notification triggered yet; each warehouse is evaluated independently (700/1000 = 70% for ANALYTICS_WH, 200/1000 = 20% for REPORTING_WH)
Two separate notifications triggered, one for each warehouse as they are tracked independently
Notification triggered; the combined consumption is 900 credits (900/1000 = 90%), which exceeds the 80% threshold
No notification triggered; resource monitors only count credits from the warehouse that consumes the most

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. When a single resource monitor is assigned to multiple warehouses, credits from all assigned warehouses are pooled toward the single shared quota. ANALYTICS_WH (700) + REPORTING_WH (200) = 900 credits total. 900/1000 = 90%, which exceeds the 80% NOTIFY threshold.

**A** is incorrect. The warehouses are not evaluated independently — they share a single credit pool against the monitor's quota.

**B** is incorrect. There are not two separate quotas; there is one shared quota. Only one notification is issued for the combined total.

**D** is incorrect. Resource monitors count all credits from all assigned warehouses, not just the highest consumer.

[Resource Monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: A new table is created by ROLE_A in the ANALYTICS schema. ROLE_B needs to query this table. What is the MINIMUM set of privileges ROLE_B requires?

options.text: SELECT on the table only
USAGE on the database, USAGE on the schema, and SELECT on the table
USAGE on the database and SELECT on the table
OWNERSHIP on the table

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Snowflake requires USAGE at every level of the object hierarchy. To query a table: USAGE on the database to access it, USAGE on the schema to navigate into it, and SELECT on the table to read rows. Missing any one causes an 'object does not exist or access denied' error.

**A** is incorrect. SELECT alone without the container USAGEs is insufficient — Snowflake will return an access denied error.

**C** is incorrect. USAGE on the database without USAGE on the schema is still insufficient — schema-level USAGE is required to access objects within the schema.

**D** is incorrect. OWNERSHIP is far more than the minimum necessary — it grants full control including the ability to grant privileges to others.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to grant a role the ability to create objects in a schema WITHOUT giving the role full ownership of the schema. Which privilege achieves this?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;
GRANT CREATE TABLE ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;
GRANT MODIFY ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. GRANT CREATE TABLE ON SCHEMA (or CREATE VIEW, CREATE STAGE, etc.) grants the ability to create specific object types within the schema without granting schema ownership. Multiple CREATE privileges can be combined to provide broader object creation capability.

**A** is incorrect. GRANT USAGE allows navigating into the schema and accessing existing objects, but it does not grant the ability to create new objects.

**C** is incorrect. GRANT MODIFY on a schema changes schema-level properties (like comments or data retention settings), not the ability to create objects.

**D** is incorrect. GRANT OWNERSHIP transfers full control of the schema, which is excessive — the role would then be able to grant and revoke privileges on the schema, not just create objects.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A security team requires that all PII columns in the database are masked for users without the COMPLIANCE_ROLE. The masking policy should be applied to 50 tables across 5 schemas. Select TWO efficient approaches to apply the masking policy at scale. (Select TWO)

options.text: Write a stored procedure that queries INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS for columns with a naming convention (e.g., ending in '_PII') and programmatically applies the masking policy using ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN SET MASKING POLICY
Use a TAG-based masking policy: create a tag (e.g., PII_TAG), apply the tag to all PII columns, and attach the masking policy to the tag using ALTER TAG SET MASKING POLICY
Create one masking policy per table and apply each individually using separate ALTER TABLE statements for each column
Grant the masking policy at the schema level to automatically apply to all current and future columns

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. A stored procedure that queries INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS for columns matching a naming convention and programmatically applies masking policies via ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN SET MASKING POLICY is a scalable automated approach.

**B** is correct. Tag-based masking is the most scalable approach: attaching a tag like 'PII' to any column automatically applies the associated masking policy — including future columns when the tag is assigned.

**C** is incorrect. Creating one masking policy per table and applying each individually is tedious and does not scale — it also creates unnecessary policy proliferation.

**D** is incorrect. There is no schema-level masking policy grant that auto-applies to all columns — this is not a valid Snowflake feature.

[Introduction to Column-Level Security](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator runs the following command: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE mydb TO ROLE reporting_role; A new table is created in mydb three days later. Does reporting_role have SELECT on the new table?

options.text: Yes, because the GRANT ON ALL TABLES grant applies to all current and future tables
No, because GRANT ON ALL TABLES is a one-time bulk grant to existing tables only; it does not create a future grant
Yes, but only if the new table is created by SYSADMIN or a role reporting_role can inherit from
No, because bulk grants at the database level are not supported in Snowflake

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE is a one-time bulk grant covering only tables that exist at the moment the command runs. Tables created after this command do NOT automatically receive the SELECT grant. A separate GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES must also be run.

**A** is incorrect. GRANT ON ALL TABLES is retrospective only — 'ALL' refers to all currently existing tables, not future ones. It does not create a future grant.

**C** is incorrect. Whether the new table is created by SYSADMIN or any other role has no bearing on whether a pre-existing grant applies to it.

**D** is incorrect. Bulk grants at the database level are fully supported in Snowflake — the issue is the one-time vs. future distinction.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: An administrator creates a row access policy that restricts data based on the user's assigned regions in a mapping table. During testing, ACCOUNTADMIN queries the table and sees no rows. Select TWO reasons this behavior is expected and what should be done. (Select TWO)

options.text: Row access policies apply to all roles including ACCOUNTADMIN; ACCOUNTADMIN is not exempt
ACCOUNTADMIN's username is likely not in the region_mapping table, so the policy returns FALSE for all rows
The fix is to add an explicit check in the policy: CURRENT_ROLE() = 'ACCOUNTADMIN' OR (mapping table condition)
The fix is to run ALTER TABLE ... ALTER ROW ACCESS POLICY BYPASS ROLES = ('ACCOUNTADMIN')

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Row access policies in Snowflake apply universally to all roles including ACCOUNTADMIN. There is no automatic bypass for any role.

**B** is correct as a diagnosis: ACCOUNTADMIN's username is likely not in the region_mapping table, so the policy evaluates to FALSE for every row and returns zero rows. Adding ACCOUNTADMIN to the mapping table (or adding a role-based bypass in the policy) resolves the issue.

**C** is correct as the fix. Adding an explicit check in the policy body — such as CASE WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'ACCOUNTADMIN' THEN TRUE ELSE EXISTS(mapping table lookup) END — is the standard pattern for granting privileged roles full visibility.

**D** is incorrect. ALTER TABLE ... ALTER ROW ACCESS POLICY BYPASS ROLES is not valid Snowflake syntax for row access policies.

[Row Access Policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-row-intro)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to verify which network policy is currently applied to the account. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES;
SELECT CURRENT_NETWORK_POLICY();
DESCRIBE ACCOUNT;
SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'NETWORK_POLICY' IN ACCOUNT;

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'NETWORK_POLICY' IN ACCOUNT returns the parameter's current value, default value, and whether it was explicitly set at the account level — directly showing which network policy (if any) is applied to the account.

**A** is incorrect. SHOW NETWORK POLICIES lists all defined network policies but does not indicate which one is currently applied at the account level.

**B** is incorrect. SELECT CURRENT_NETWORK_POLICY() is not a valid Snowflake function.

**C** is incorrect. DESCRIBE ACCOUNT is not a valid Snowflake command — account metadata is accessed via SHOW PARAMETERS or SHOW ORGANIZATION ACCOUNTS.

[Network Policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator notices that a network policy blocks connections from a specific IP that a vendor needs to use. The account policy is managed by a different team and cannot be changed quickly. The vendor's user account is VENDOR_USER. What is the fastest solution to allow the vendor access?

options.text: Create a new network policy that includes the vendor's IP and assign it to VENDOR_USER using ALTER USER VENDOR_USER SET NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy
Ask the vendor to use a VPN to access Snowflake from an approved IP range
Temporarily disable the account network policy using ALTER ACCOUNT UNSET NETWORK_POLICY
Add the vendor's IP to the ALLOWED_IP_LIST on the existing account network policy

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. A user-level network policy overrides the account-level network policy for that specific user. Creating a vendor-specific policy that includes the vendor's IP and assigning it directly to VENDOR_USER is the fastest targeted solution that does not affect other users.

**B** is incorrect. Asking the vendor to use a VPN requires coordination with the vendor and does not involve any Snowflake configuration change.

**C** is incorrect. Temporarily disabling the account network policy removes IP-based protection for all users — a serious security risk.

**D** is incorrect. The account network policy is managed by a different team and cannot be changed quickly — this option is ruled out by the scenario.

[Network Policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A company using Azure is implementing Azure Private Link for Snowflake. Select TWO accurate statements about this implementation. (Select TWO)

options.text: Azure Private Link for Snowflake requires Business Critical edition or higher
After enabling Private Link, the public internet endpoint for the Snowflake account is automatically disabled
Clients must update their connection strings to use the Private Link-specific hostname
Azure Private Link works across different Azure regions — the VNET and Snowflake account do not need to be in the same region

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Azure Private Link for Snowflake is only available on Business Critical edition or higher.

**B** is incorrect. The public internet endpoint is NOT automatically disabled when Private Link is enabled — both endpoints coexist by default. Administrators must explicitly enforce Private Link-only access if desired.

**C** is correct. Clients must update their connection strings to use the Private Link-specific hostname (which includes 'privatelink' in the URL). The standard public endpoint URL still routes through the internet.

**D** is incorrect. Azure Private Link requires the VNET and the Snowflake account to be in the same Azure region — cross-region private connectivity is not supported.

[Network Policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is setting up external access integration for a Python UDF that needs to call an external REST API. The company's security policy requires that only specific external domains be allowed. What is the correct Snowflake construct to enforce this?

options.text: Create a network policy with ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('<api_domain_ip>') and assign it to the UDF's owner role
Create a NETWORK RULE with TYPE = HOST and VALUE_LIST = ('api.example.com'), then include it in an EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION
Set ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOWED_EXTERNAL_DOMAINS = ('api.example.com')
Create a network policy with TYPE = EGRESS and add the domain to ALLOWED_DOMAINS

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. For controlling egress from Snowflake UDFs to external services, the correct approach uses Network Rules (TYPE = HOST for domain-based rules) combined with External Access Integrations. The EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION ties together the network rule and optional secrets, and is referenced in the UDF definition.

**A** is incorrect. Network policies (with ALLOWED_IP_LIST) control inbound access to Snowflake — they do not govern outbound egress from UDFs.

**C** is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOWED_EXTERNAL_DOMAINS is not a valid Snowflake account parameter.

**D** is incorrect. Network policies do not have a TYPE = EGRESS parameter or an ALLOWED_DOMAINS list — these concepts apply to Network Rules, not network policies.

[Network Rules](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-rules)

questions.stem: A warehouse is configured with SIZE = 'X-LARGE', AUTO_SUSPEND = 300, and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE. If the warehouse has been idle for 6 minutes (360 seconds), what is its current state?

options.text: Running but idle, because auto-suspend has not yet triggered
Suspended, but it will automatically resume every 30 minutes to check for queued queries
Running, because X-LARGE warehouses have a minimum runtime of 10 minutes before auto-suspend
Suspended, because 360 seconds of idle time exceeds the AUTO_SUSPEND threshold of 300 seconds

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. AUTO_SUSPEND = 300 means the warehouse suspends after 300 seconds (5 minutes) of inactivity. With 360 seconds (6 minutes) of idle time, the threshold is exceeded and the warehouse is suspended.

**A** is incorrect. The 5-minute threshold has been exceeded, so the warehouse would have already suspended.

**C** is incorrect. Warehouse size does not affect the auto-suspend timing — X-LARGE warehouses behave identically to SMALL warehouses with respect to auto-suspend.

**B** is incorrect. Suspended warehouses do not periodically wake up to check for queued queries — they remain suspended until a query is submitted and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE triggers a restart.

[Virtual Warehouses Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to enable the Query Acceleration Service for a specific warehouse to help with ad-hoc analytical queries that occasionally scan very large datasets. The service should not scale beyond 4x the warehouse's compute for any single query. Which command configures this?

options.text: ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION = TRUE QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR = 4;
ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET QUERY_ACCELERATION = ENABLED MAX_SCALE = 4;
CREATE QUERY ACCELERATION POLICY qa_policy MAX_SCALE = 4; ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET QUERY_ACCELERATION_POLICY = qa_policy;
ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION = TRUE; ALTER ACCOUNT SET QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR = 4;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. ALTER WAREHOUSE ... SET ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION = TRUE QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR = 4 enables the Query Acceleration Service and caps the scale factor at 4x. Both parameters can be set in a single ALTER WAREHOUSE statement. The default MAX_SCALE_FACTOR is 8 if not specified.

**B** is incorrect. QUERY_ACCELERATION = ENABLED and MAX_SCALE = 4 are not valid Snowflake warehouse parameter names.

**C** is incorrect. There is no QUERY ACCELERATION POLICY object in Snowflake — this construct does not exist.

**D** is incorrect. QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR is a warehouse-level parameter, not an account-level parameter. Setting it at the account level via ALTER ACCOUNT is not supported.

[Query Acceleration Service](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/query-acceleration-service)

questions.stem: A DBA is evaluating the storage costs for a Snowflake account on Enterprise edition. Select TWO true statements about how storage is billed in Snowflake. (Select TWO)

options.text: Time Travel storage (historical versions of modified or deleted data) is billed at the same rate as active storage
Fail-Safe storage (the 7-day Snowflake-managed protection period) is charged to the customer in addition to Time Travel storage
Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on a table immediately frees up Time Travel storage and reduces costs
Cloning a table creates an independent copy of all data immediately, doubling the storage cost for the cloned table

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Time Travel storage (historical versions of modified or deleted data) is billed at the same per-TB rate as active storage.

**B** is correct. Fail-Safe provides an additional 7 days of Snowflake-managed data protection and is also billed to the customer — total protected storage can include active data + Time Travel + Fail-Safe.

**C** is incorrect. Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS does not immediately purge historical versions — Snowflake's background processes eventually clean up, but the change is not instant.

**D** is incorrect. Table cloning in Snowflake uses zero-copy cloning — no data is physically duplicated at clone time, so storage cost is not doubled immediately. Only divergent (subsequently changed) data incurs additional storage.

[Fail-Safe](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe)

questions.stem: An administrator is enabling automatic clustering on a very large table (10 TB) with a cluster key of (REGION, ORDER_DATE). The table receives heavy INSERT activity throughout the day. What ongoing cost consideration should the administrator communicate to stakeholders?

options.text: Automatic clustering is free because it uses idle warehouse compute cycles
Automatic clustering charges a one-time fee at the time the CLUSTER BY key is defined
Automatic clustering consumes serverless credits continuously as Snowflake reclusters micro-partitions in the background, charged separately from warehouse credits
Automatic clustering uses the warehouse's existing compute and is included in the warehouse's credit consumption

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Automatic clustering is a serverless feature — it uses Snowflake-managed background compute, not the customer's virtual warehouses. Credits are charged continuously as the service reclusters micro-partitions. For a large, heavily-modified table these charges can be significant.

**A** is incorrect. Automatic clustering is not free — it has its own serverless credit billing entirely separate from warehouse billing.

**B** is incorrect. There is no one-time fee for defining a CLUSTER BY key — charges are ongoing and based on the clustering work performed.

**D** is incorrect. Automatic clustering does not use existing warehouse compute — it runs on fully separate Snowflake-managed serverless infrastructure.

[Micro-partitions and Clustering](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions)

questions.stem: A CISO requests a report of all queries that accessed the PATIENTS table containing PHI data in the last 6 months, including the user, timestamp, and specific columns accessed. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and approach best satisfies this request?

options.text: Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY WHERE QUERY_TEXT ILIKE '%PATIENTS%' to find queries referencing the table
Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY WHERE DIRECT_OBJECTS_ACCESSED LIKE '%PATIENTS%' to get structured access records with column-level detail
Query SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY for the past 6 months of query logs
Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY to see all users who logged in during that period

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. ACCESS_HISTORY contains structured records of which objects (tables, views) and columns were accessed by each query, captured by user, role, timestamp, and query ID. DIRECT_OBJECTS_ACCESSED and BASE_OBJECTS_ACCESSED are array columns containing table and column names — providing the precise column-level audit trail needed for PHI compliance.

**A** is incorrect. Text searching QUERY_HISTORY for table name mentions is unreliable — queries may use aliases, views, or dynamic SQL that obscures the actual table being accessed.

**C** is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY only retains data for 7 days, which is insufficient for a 6-month compliance requirement.

**D** is incorrect. LOGIN_HISTORY tracks authentication events, not data access — it shows who logged in but not what data they queried.

[Access History](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/access-history)

questions.stem: A security team needs to identify all privilege grants that occurred in the past 30 days, including who granted them and to whom. INFORMATION_SCHEMA does not retain sufficient history. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and columns provide this information?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES — columns CREATED_ON, PRIVILEGE, GRANTED_ON, NAME, GRANTEE_NAME, GRANTED_BY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_USERS — columns CREATED_ON, ROLE, GRANTEE_NAME, GRANTED_BY
Both GRANTS_TO_ROLES and GRANTS_TO_USERS should be queried to capture all privilege grant activity
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered for QUERY_TYPE = 'GRANT'

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Privilege grants in Snowflake are of two types: object privileges granted to roles (captured in GRANTS_TO_ROLES) and role grants to users (GRANTS_TO_USERS). To capture all grant activity from the past 30 days, both views must be queried. Both views retain data for 1 year.

**A** is incorrect on its own. GRANTS_TO_ROLES captures object-privilege grants (SELECT, INSERT, USAGE, etc.) but does not capture role grants to users — querying only this view misses half the picture.

**B** is incorrect on its own. GRANTS_TO_USERS captures role assignments to users but not object-privilege grants to roles.

**D** is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY filtered for QUERY_TYPE = 'GRANT' could work but provides raw SQL text without structured privilege metadata, making it much harder to analyze.

[Account Usage Schema](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is configuring data masking for a financial application. The CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER column should show the full number to FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE and a masked version (showing only last 4 digits) to all other roles. Select TWO valid ways to implement this conditional masking logic in the policy. (Select TWO)

options.text: CASE WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE' THEN val ELSE CONCAT('XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-', RIGHT(val, 4)) END
IFF(IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE'), val, CONCAT('XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-', RIGHT(val, 4)))
CASE WHEN CURRENT_USER() IN (SELECT username FROM fraud_analyst_users) THEN val ELSE 'XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX' END
GRANT UNMASK ON COLUMN credit_card_number TO ROLE FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE — no masking policy needed

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. CASE WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE' THEN val ELSE CONCAT('XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-', RIGHT(val, 4)) END is the standard pattern for role-based masking, checking the currently active primary role.

**B** is correct. IFF(IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE'), val, ...) uses IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() which checks whether the role is active in the session — including as a secondary role — making it broader and more robust for users who may have the role as either primary or secondary.

**C** is incorrect. Using a subquery against a user lookup table inside a masking policy is a possible but discouraged pattern. It mixes user identity with role-based access control and creates a maintenance dependency on an external table.

**D** is incorrect. GRANT UNMASK is not a valid Snowflake privilege — masking is controlled entirely through policy logic, not through privilege grants.

[Introduction to Column-Level Security](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro)

questions.stem: A regulated financial services company requires that Snowflake activity logs be retained for 7 years for compliance purposes, but ACCOUNT_USAGE only retains data for 1 year. What architectural approach should the administrator implement?

options.text: Contact Snowflake support to extend ACCOUNT_USAGE retention to 7 years for an additional fee
Create Snowflake Tasks that periodically query ACCOUNT_USAGE views and INSERT results into customer-managed tables (compliance archive tables) with no time limit
Enable the Data Retention Extension feature in account settings to extend ACCOUNT_USAGE to 7 years
Use Snowflake's built-in compliance mode which automatically retains all audit logs for 7 years

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The standard architectural pattern for long-term audit log retention is to use Snowflake Tasks to regularly extract ACCOUNT_USAGE data (LOGIN_HISTORY, QUERY_HISTORY, ACCESS_HISTORY, GRANTS_TO_ROLES, etc.) and insert it into customer-managed tables within the account or export to external storage. Customer-managed tables have configurable retention.

**A** is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE views have a fixed 1-year retention that cannot be extended regardless of Snowflake support requests.

**C** is incorrect. There is no Data Retention Extension feature for ACCOUNT_USAGE — the 1-year limit is a system-level constraint.

**D** is incorrect. Snowflake does not have a built-in 7-year compliance mode that automatically retains all audit logs.

[Tasks](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tasks-intro)

questions.stem: An administrator wants to set a hard limit on how long any transaction can hold locks on resources in the account, to prevent long-running uncommitted transactions from blocking other operations. Which parameter should be configured at the account level?

options.text: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS
TRANSACTION_ABORT_ON_ERROR
LOCK_TIMEOUT
QUERY_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. LOCK_TIMEOUT controls the maximum number of seconds a statement will wait to acquire a lock before returning an error. Setting this at the account level establishes an account-wide default that prevents indefinite lock waits and cascading blocking.

**A** is incorrect. STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS limits total query execution time — not specifically lock wait time. A query could acquire its lock quickly but still run for a long time.

**B** is incorrect. TRANSACTION_ABORT_ON_ERROR controls whether a transaction rolls back when a statement within it errors — it does not control lock wait duration.

**D** is incorrect. QUERY_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS is not a standard Snowflake account parameter name — the correct parameter for statement execution time is STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS.

[Parameters](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/parameters)

questions.stem: An administrator wants to verify whether the STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS parameter has been explicitly set at the account level or is using the system default. Which command provides this distinction?

options.text: SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'STATEMENT_TIMEOUT%' IN ACCOUNT;
SELECT CURRENT_PARAMETERS();
DESCRIBE PARAMETER STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS;
SHOW ACCOUNT PARAMETERS CURRENT;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'STATEMENT_TIMEOUT%' IN ACCOUNT returns rows including the current value, default value, type, and a 'level' column that shows whether the parameter was set at the ACCOUNT level or is using the SYSTEM DEFAULT. This distinction tells the administrator whether an explicit account-level override exists.

**B** is incorrect. SELECT CURRENT_PARAMETERS() is not a valid Snowflake function.

**C** is incorrect. DESCRIBE PARAMETER is not valid Snowflake syntax — SHOW PARAMETERS is the correct command.

**D** is incorrect. SHOW ACCOUNT PARAMETERS CURRENT is not valid Snowflake syntax.

[Parameters](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/parameters)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to unlock a service account that was locked after repeated failed key pair authentication attempts. Which command accomplishes this without enabling password login?

options.text: ALTER USER svc_account RESET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY;
ALTER USER svc_account SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0;
ALTER USER svc_account SET DISABLED = FALSE;
DROP USER svc_account; CREATE USER svc_account RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<key>';

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. ALTER USER svc_account SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0 immediately clears the lockout state for the user without changing the authentication method or enabling password login. The user retains their key pair authentication configuration.

**A** is incorrect. RESET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY is not valid Snowflake syntax — the correct parameter name for key rotation is RSA_PUBLIC_KEY or RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2.

**C** is incorrect. SET DISABLED = FALSE re-enables a manually disabled user account but does not clear a login lock caused by failed authentication attempts — these are different states.

**D** is incorrect. Dropping and recreating the user would clear the lock but would lose all grants, role memberships, and other user properties.

[ALTER USER](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/alter-user)

questions.stem: A new data engineer joins the team. The administrator needs to configure their account so that upon login they automatically use the DATA_ENGINEER_ROLE role and the DE_WH warehouse, and their default database/schema context is set to PROD_DB.STAGING. Select TWO properties that configure this experience. (Select TWO)

options.text: DEFAULT_ROLE = 'DATA_ENGINEER_ROLE' and DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE = 'DE_WH'
DEFAULT_NAMESPACE = 'PROD_DB.STAGING' — sets the default database and schema context
SESSION_ROLE = 'DATA_ENGINEER_ROLE' and SESSION_WAREHOUSE = 'DE_WH'
DEFAULT_DATABASE = 'PROD_DB' and DEFAULT_SCHEMA = 'STAGING'

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. DEFAULT_ROLE and DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE are the correct Snowflake user properties for setting the automatic role and warehouse at session start.

**B** is correct. DEFAULT_NAMESPACE = 'DATABASE.SCHEMA' is the correct property name for setting the default database and schema context combined — specified as a dot-separated namespace string.

**C** is incorrect. SESSION_ROLE and SESSION_WAREHOUSE are not valid Snowflake user properties — these are not valid ALTER USER parameters.

**D** is incorrect. While setting DEFAULT_DATABASE and DEFAULT_SCHEMA separately might seem logical, Snowflake uses DEFAULT_NAMESPACE for the combined database.schema context. Separately setting these two properties is not the correct Snowflake syntax.

[ALTER USER](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/alter-user)

questions.stem: A Snowflake governance team is using role COMMENT fields to document ownership. They need to update the comment on SALES_DATA_ROLE to reflect the new owning team. The role currently has comment 'Old Sales Team'. What command updates it?

options.text: UPDATE ROLE sales_data_role SET COMMENT = 'New Sales Operations Team';
ALTER ROLE sales_data_role SET COMMENT = 'New Sales Operations Team';
MODIFY ROLE sales_data_role COMMENT = 'New Sales Operations Team';
CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE sales_data_role COMMENT = 'New Sales Operations Team';

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. ALTER ROLE role_name SET COMMENT = 'text' is the correct Snowflake DDL command to update the comment on an existing role. The comment is visible in SHOW ROLES output and in the Snowflake web UI.

**A** is incorrect. UPDATE ROLE is not valid Snowflake DDL syntax — UPDATE is a DML operation for table rows, not a DDL command for schema objects.

**C** is incorrect. MODIFY ROLE is not valid Snowflake syntax.

**D** is incorrect. CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE would drop and recreate the role, destroying all existing role grants and memberships — highly destructive for a simple comment update.

[ALTER ROLE](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/alter-role)

questions.stem: Multiple Snowflake tasks call the same stored procedure. Inside the procedure, different cleanup logic should run depending on which task invoked it. Which function enables this task-aware branching?

options.text: CURRENT_STATEMENT()
SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME()
SESSION_CONTEXT('task')
GET_TASK_ID()

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME() returns the fully-qualified name of the Snowflake task that is currently executing the stored procedure. By comparing this value in an IF/CASE statement, the procedure can branch to different logic depending on which task invoked it.

**A** is incorrect. CURRENT_STATEMENT() returns the SQL text of the executing statement — not the name of the calling task.

**C** is incorrect. SESSION_CONTEXT('task') is not a valid Snowflake function.

**D** is incorrect. GET_TASK_ID() is not a valid Snowflake system function.

[SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/functions/system_current_user_task_name)

questions.stem: A Snowflake deployment script needs to create multiple roles without failing if some roles already exist from previous deployments. Which syntax makes each CREATE ROLE statement idempotent?

options.text: CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS role_name;
CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE role_name;
MERGE INTO ROLES VALUES (role_name);
INSERT ROLE IF ABSENT role_name;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS creates the role only if it does not already exist. If the role exists, no error is raised and no changes are made to the existing role or its grants — making it safe to run repeatedly in deployment scripts.

**B** is incorrect. CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE drops the existing role and creates a new one, destroying all grants and role memberships — never acceptable in an idempotent deployment script.

**C** is incorrect. MERGE INTO ROLES is not valid Snowflake syntax — MERGE is a DML operation for tables, not applicable to role management.

**D** is incorrect. INSERT ROLE IF ABSENT is not valid Snowflake syntax.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: An administrator wants to audit all roles in a Snowflake account and filter the results to show only roles whose names contain the string 'DATA'. Which is the correct filtering syntax?

options.text: LIST ROLES WHERE NAME CONTAINS 'DATA';
SHOW ROLES CONTAINING 'DATA';
SHOW ROLES LIKE '%DATA%';
SELECT * FROM ROLES WHERE ROLE_NAME CONTAINS 'DATA';

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. SHOW ROLES LIKE '%DATA%' uses SQL LIKE wildcard pattern matching to filter results — the % wildcard matches zero or more characters, so '%DATA%' returns all roles containing 'DATA' anywhere in the name.

**B** is incorrect. SHOW ROLES CONTAINING is not valid Snowflake syntax.

**A** is incorrect. LIST ROLES is not a valid Snowflake command — SHOW ROLES is the correct command.

**D** is incorrect. Querying SELECT * FROM ROLES without the SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE prefix is not valid, and even the ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES view would require different syntax (ILIKE not CONTAINS).

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator has been notified that behavior change bundle '2024_07' is scheduled to become mandatory next quarter. They want to proactively test whether any existing queries or procedures break under the new behavior. Which Snowflake function enables the bundle for testing in a dev account?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE_ENABLED = '2024_07';
SELECT SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('2024_07');
SET PARAMETER BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE = '2024_07';
CREATE BEHAVIOR CHANGE TRIAL FOR BUNDLE '2024_07';

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. SELECT SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('2024_07') is the correct Snowflake system function to opt an account into a specific behavior change bundle before it is automatically enforced, enabling proactive testing.

**A** is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT SET BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE_ENABLED is not a valid Snowflake account parameter.

**C** is incorrect. SET PARAMETER BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE is not valid Snowflake syntax.

**D** is incorrect. CREATE BEHAVIOR CHANGE TRIAL is not a valid Snowflake construct.

[Tasks](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tasks-intro)

questions.stem: A company assigns resource monitors to individual warehouses. Warehouse A has its own resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 200. Warehouse A also falls under the account-level resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 5000. When Warehouse A consumes 200 credits, which monitor's trigger fires?

options.text: Only the warehouse-level monitor fires; it takes precedence over the account-level monitor for Warehouse A
Both monitors fire simultaneously when Warehouse A reaches 200 credits
Only the account-level monitor fires because it has a higher quota
Warehouse A's credits count toward both monitors independently

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. When a warehouse has its own resource monitor assigned, that warehouse-level monitor takes precedence. Credits from warehouses with dedicated monitors are excluded from the account-level monitor's tracking. Warehouse A is governed exclusively by its own 200-credit monitor.

**B** is incorrect. Both monitors do not fire simultaneously for the same warehouse — the warehouse-level monitor takes precedence and the account-level monitor does not count credits from that warehouse at all.

**C** is incorrect. The account-level monitor does not track Warehouse A's credits at all when Warehouse A has its own dedicated monitor — quota size is irrelevant to the precedence rule.

**D** is incorrect. While Warehouse A's credits do count toward both monitors at the account-level, option A accurately describes the actual behavior: when a warehouse-level monitor is assigned, the account-level monitor is bypassed for that warehouse.

[Resource Monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: A resource monitor was set with NOTIFY_USERS = ('ADMIN_A') and a NOTIFY trigger at 80%. ADMIN_A left the company and their account was dropped. The monitor still has ADMIN_A in NOTIFY_USERS. What happens when 80% is reached?

options.text: The resource monitor is automatically disabled because the NOTIFY_USERS list contains an invalid user
All ACCOUNTADMIN users receive the notification instead as a fallback
Snowflake raises an error and the NOTIFY trigger does not fire
The notification is silently skipped because the user no longer exists; the monitor continues to track credits normally

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Resource monitor notifications are sent on a best-effort basis. If a user in NOTIFY_USERS no longer exists or has no valid email, the notification for that user is silently skipped. The monitor continues to track credits and fire trigger actions normally.

**A** is incorrect. A stale user reference in NOTIFY_USERS does not disable the resource monitor — monitoring and other trigger actions (SUSPEND, SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY) continue to function.

**C** is incorrect. Snowflake does not raise an error when a NOTIFY trigger fires for a nonexistent user — the notification simply fails silently.

**B** is incorrect. There is no fallback mechanism that routes notifications to ACCOUNTADMIN users when a NOTIFY_USERS entry becomes invalid.

[Resource Monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: An administrator needs to increase the monthly credit quota on a resource monitor from 500 to 800 credits in the middle of the current monitoring period. Which command achieves this without resetting the usage counter?

options.text: ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 800;
DROP RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor; CREATE RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor WITH CREDIT_QUOTA = 800;
ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor ADD 300 TO CREDIT_QUOTA;
UPDATE RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 800;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 800 updates the quota value mid-period without resetting the CREDITS_USED counter. The monitor continues tracking from its current position, now measured against the new 800-credit quota.

**B** is incorrect. Dropping and recreating the resource monitor would reset CREDITS_USED to zero, losing tracking continuity for the current period.

**C** is incorrect. ADD N TO CREDIT_QUOTA is not valid Snowflake syntax for ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR.

**D** is incorrect. UPDATE RESOURCE MONITOR is not valid Snowflake syntax — UPDATE is a DML operation for table rows.

[Resource Monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: An administrator wants to identify which virtual warehouses are experiencing the most query queuing during business hours. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides warehouse-level load metrics at regular time intervals?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.RESOURCE_USAGE_HISTORY

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING provides 5-minute interval snapshots of each virtual warehouse's load state, including RUNNING, QUEUED, and BLOCKED query counts. This is the purpose-built view for identifying queuing pressure by warehouse and time of day.

**A** is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY tracks credit consumption per hour — it shows how much compute was used, not how many queries were queued.

**B** is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY has per-query wait time data but requires complex aggregation to identify warehouse-level queuing patterns and does not provide pre-aggregated load snapshots.

**D** is incorrect. RESOURCE_USAGE_HISTORY is not a standard ACCOUNT_USAGE view name in Snowflake.

[Virtual Warehouses Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is preparing a monthly storage cost report. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides daily total storage usage broken down by AVERAGE_DATABASE_BYTES, AVERAGE_STAGE_BYTES, and AVERAGE_FAILSAFE_BYTES?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATABASE_STORAGE_USAGE_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE provides daily account-level snapshots with AVERAGE_DATABASE_BYTES (data in all databases), AVERAGE_STAGE_BYTES (data in stages), and AVERAGE_FAILSAFE_BYTES (Fail-Safe storage). This view retains data for 1 year and is ideal for monthly storage cost reporting.

**A** is incorrect. TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS provides per-table storage detail but is a current point-in-time view — it does not retain historical daily snapshots needed for cost trend reporting.

**C** is incorrect. DATABASE_STORAGE_USAGE_HISTORY provides database-level breakdowns but not the account-level AVERAGE_STAGE_BYTES and AVERAGE_FAILSAFE_BYTES breakdown the question specifies.

**D** is incorrect. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY tracks credit consumption by service type — not storage bytes.

[Fail-Safe](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe)

questions.stem: An administrator needs to understand the credit cost of serverless Snowpipe ingestion and serverless task execution separately. Which TWO ACCOUNT_USAGE views provide this per-service breakdown? (Select TWO)

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY — for Snowpipe credit consumption per named pipe
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY — for serverless task credit consumption per task
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY — provides combined daily totals for all serverless services
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — includes a SERVICE_TYPE column for Snowpipe and tasks

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY provides per-pipe Snowpipe credit consumption, enabling cost attribution to individual ingestion pipelines.

**B** is correct. SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY provides per-task execution records with credit consumption, enabling drill-down into task-specific serverless costs.

**C** is incorrect. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides aggregate daily totals by service type but does not break down to individual pipes or tasks — it cannot attribute costs to a specific named pipe or task.

**D** is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY only captures virtual warehouse credits — it does not include any serverless service costs (Snowpipe, tasks, clustering, etc.).

[Account Usage Schema](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage)

questions.stem: An administrator runs GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA finance TO ROLE analyst_role followed by GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA finance TO ROLE analyst_role. Three tables already exist. A fourth table is created two weeks later. Which tables does analyst_role have SELECT on?

options.text: Only the three original tables — future grants only apply from the moment they are run, but the first command already covered them
All tables in the database, not just the finance schema
Only the fourth table — the ALL TABLES grant expires and the FUTURE grant takes over
All four tables — the first command covers the existing three and the future grant covers the new one

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. The first command (GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA) is a point-in-time grant covering the three existing tables. The second command (GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA) creates a standing grant for all future tables, including the fourth one created two weeks later. Together they provide complete coverage.

**A** is incorrect. ALL TABLES grants do not expire — the three original tables retain the SELECT grant permanently unless explicitly revoked.

**C** is incorrect. The ALL TABLES grant does not get replaced by the FUTURE TABLES grant — both grants coexist independently, each covering different tables.

**B** is incorrect. The grants are scoped specifically to the finance schema, not to the entire database. Only tables in the finance schema are covered.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: An administrator discovers that ANALYST_ROLE was accidentally granted INSERT privilege on a sensitive production table. Only SELECT should be allowed. Which is the most precise command to correct this?

options.text: REVOKE INSERT ON TABLE prod.secure.transactions FROM ROLE analyst_role;
REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON TABLE prod.secure.transactions FROM ROLE analyst_role;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE prod.secure.transactions TO ROLE sysadmin;
DROP ROLE analyst_role; CREATE ROLE analyst_role; -- Re-grant SELECT only

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. REVOKE INSERT ON TABLE removes only the INSERT privilege from ANALYST_ROLE on that specific table. The SELECT privilege remains intact. This is the most targeted, minimum-impact correction.

**B** is incorrect. REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES would also revoke the SELECT privilege that the role should retain — too broad.

**C** is incorrect. GRANT OWNERSHIP to SYSADMIN transfers table ownership, which may revoke all existing grants on the table — far beyond what is needed for this correction.

**D** is incorrect. Dropping and recreating ANALYST_ROLE destroys all grants and memberships associated with the role — extremely destructive for a targeted privilege correction.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A stored procedure PROCESS_HR_DATA runs with EXECUTE AS OWNER. The owner has elevated access to HR tables. To prevent misuse, the procedure should only execute sensitive operations when called by users with HR_ADMIN_ROLE in their hierarchy. Which function checks this?

options.text: CURRENT_ROLE() = 'HR_ADMIN_ROLE'
IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('HR_ADMIN_ROLE')
IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('HR_ADMIN_ROLE')
INVOKER_ROLE() = 'HR_ADMIN_ROLE'

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('role_name') is specifically designed for use inside EXECUTE AS OWNER stored procedures. It checks whether the caller (invoker) has a specific role anywhere in their role hierarchy, regardless of which role is currently active in the owner's execution context.

**A** is incorrect. CURRENT_ROLE() inside an EXECUTE AS OWNER procedure returns the owner's active role — not the caller's role. The caller's identity is not reflected by CURRENT_ROLE().

**B** is incorrect. IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() checks the current session's role context. Inside EXECUTE AS OWNER, this reflects the owner's session roles, not the invoker's roles.

**D** is incorrect. INVOKER_ROLE() is not a valid Snowflake function.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A Snowflake role ETL_ROLE has been granted OWNERSHIP on a production schema. A security review identifies this as a risk. Which specific capability does OWNERSHIP on a schema grant that makes it risky for an ETL role?

options.text: The role can drop any table or view in the schema without requiring additional DROP privileges
The role can grant any privilege on any object within the schema to any other role, bypassing normal access control review
The role automatically gains ACCOUNTADMIN-level access when it owns a production schema
The role can modify the account-level time travel retention for all objects in the schema

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. OWNERSHIP of a schema allows the owning role to grant any privilege on any object within that schema to any other role — bypassing normal access control review. An ETL role with schema OWNERSHIP could escalate its own access or grant broad access to other roles, creating a privilege escalation risk.

**A** is incorrect as the primary concern. While owners can manage owned objects, the more significant security risk is the ability to grant privileges to arbitrary roles.

**C** is incorrect. Owning a schema does not grant ACCOUNTADMIN-level access — role hierarchy and object ownership are separate concepts.

**D** is incorrect. Schema owners cannot modify account-level time travel retention — that is an account parameter controlled by ACCOUNTADMIN.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: An account receives a new data share from a provider. The administrator needs to allow the DATA_ANALYSTS team role to query the shared data. The shared database is PARTNER_DB. Which privilege grant is specific to shared databases?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE partner_db TO ROLE data_analysts;
GRANT READ ON SHARE partner_db TO ROLE data_analysts;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE partner_db TO ROLE data_analysts;
GRANT IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE partner_db TO ROLE data_analysts;

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. GRANT IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE is the privilege required for roles to access objects in a database created from an inbound data share. This single grant replaces the standard USAGE + individual-object-grants pattern for shared databases.

**A** is incorrect. GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE alone is not sufficient for shared databases — shared databases require IMPORTED PRIVILEGES, not just USAGE.

**C** is incorrect. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES works on local tables but cannot be applied to shared databases — the objects in a shared database are read-only and access is governed by IMPORTED PRIVILEGES.

**B** is incorrect. GRANT READ ON SHARE is not valid Snowflake syntax — shares are not granted with a READ privilege.

[Introduction to Data Sharing](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro)

questions.stem: A data governance team wants to review all current privilege grants on a table FINANCE.REPORTING.BUDGET_SUMMARY, including who has SELECT, INSERT, and OWNERSHIP. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE finance.reporting.budget_summary;
SELECT * FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.OBJECT_PRIVILEGES WHERE TABLE_NAME = 'BUDGET_SUMMARY';
DESCRIBE TABLE finance.reporting.budget_summary PRIVILEGES;
SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE sysadmin ON TABLE finance.reporting.budget_summary;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE fully_qualified_table_name lists all privilege grants on that specific table including privilege type, grantee role name, and the GRANTED_BY column showing who issued each grant. This is the definitive command for auditing object-level grants.

**B** is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.OBJECT_PRIVILEGES can provide similar information but has only 7-day retention and is less commonly used for this purpose.

**C** is incorrect. DESCRIBE TABLE with PRIVILEGES is not valid Snowflake syntax — DESCRIBE TABLE shows column definitions, not privilege grants.

**D** is incorrect. SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE shows all privileges held by a specific role across all objects — it does not show all grants on a specific object from all grantees.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A Snowflake Native App installed in a consumer account defines Application Roles APP_VIEWER and APP_CONTRIBUTOR. Select TWO true statements about how these Application Roles work in the consumer account. (Select TWO)

options.text: Application Roles are granted to account roles in the consumer account — not directly to users
Application Roles can be listed with SHOW APPLICATION ROLES IN APPLICATION <app_name>
Application Roles are identical to regular account roles and appear in SHOW ROLES output
The consumer account can modify the privileges assigned to Application Roles

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Application Roles are granted to account roles in the consumer account, not directly to users. The syntax is GRANT APPLICATION ROLE app_name.APP_VIEWER TO ROLE my_account_role.

**B** is correct. SHOW APPLICATION ROLES IN APPLICATION app_name lists the Application Roles available for a specific installed app in the consumer account.

**C** is incorrect. Application Roles are separate from account roles and do NOT appear in regular SHOW ROLES output — they are scoped to their application object.

**D** is incorrect. The consumer account cannot modify the privileges assigned to Application Roles — those are defined entirely by the app provider in the application package.

[About the Snowflake Native App Framework](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/native-apps-about)

questions.stem: A Snowflake account has a network policy applied at the account level. An administrator needs to apply a different, more permissive network policy to a single API service account user API_SVC. Which command applies a user-level network policy override?

options.text: ALTER USER api_svc SET NETWORK_POLICY = api_svc_policy;
ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY = api_svc_policy FOR USER api_svc;
GRANT NETWORK_POLICY api_svc_policy TO USER api_svc;
CREATE NETWORK POLICY OVERRIDE FOR USER api_svc USING api_svc_policy;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. User-level network policies are assigned using ALTER USER user_name SET NETWORK_POLICY = policy_name. A policy set at the user level fully overrides the account-level policy for that specific user, while the account-level policy continues to govern all other users.

**B** is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY with a FOR USER clause is not valid Snowflake syntax — account-level and user-level policies are set with separate commands.

**C** is incorrect. GRANT NETWORK_POLICY is not a valid Snowflake command — network policies are not privileges that are granted.

**D** is incorrect. CREATE NETWORK POLICY OVERRIDE FOR USER is not a valid Snowflake construct.

[Network Policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: An administrator needs to review the IP allowlist and blocklist for a specific network policy named CORP_POLICY. Which command shows the details of this specific policy?

options.text: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES LIKE 'CORP_POLICY';
DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy;
SELECT * FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.NETWORK_POLICIES WHERE POLICY_NAME = 'CORP_POLICY';
SELECT ALLOWED_IP_LIST, BLOCKED_IP_LIST FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.NETWORK_POLICIES WHERE NAME = 'CORP_POLICY';

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy provides the full detail of the specific policy including the complete ALLOWED_IP_LIST and BLOCKED_IP_LIST entries. This is the command to use when you need to inspect the actual IP ranges configured in the policy.

**A** is incorrect for this purpose. SHOW NETWORK POLICIES LIKE filters the list to matching policies and shows summary information (IP list entry counts) but does not display the actual IP addresses in the lists.

**C** is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA does not have a NETWORK_POLICIES view in Snowflake.

**D** is incorrect. SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.NETWORK_POLICIES is not a valid account usage view — network policy details are not accessible through this schema.

[Network Policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator receives reports that an external stage backed by Google Cloud Storage (GCS) is returning permission errors. Which Snowflake function can be used to diagnose the connectivity and access permission issues with this external stage?

options.text: SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('@my_gcs_stage')
SYSTEM$VALIDATE_GCS_INTEGRATION('my_gcs_integration')
LIST @my_gcs_stage — if it returns results, the stage connection is working
SELECT STAGE_STATUS FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGES WHERE STAGE_NAME = 'MY_GCS_STAGE';

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('@stage_name') tests connectivity to an external stage and validates that Snowflake has the necessary permissions to list, read, and write to the underlying cloud storage. It returns diagnostic output indicating success or the specific failure reason.

**B** is incorrect. SYSTEM$VALIDATE_GCS_INTEGRATION is not a valid Snowflake system function.

**C** is incorrect. LIST @stage will show whether listing works but returns only file names and sizes — it does not provide structured diagnostic output when there are permission failures.

**D** is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGES does not have a real-time connectivity status column — it only shows stage configuration metadata.

[Network Rules](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-rules)

questions.stem: A company uses Snowflake on GCP with Private Service Connect enabled. They have an IP-based network policy with ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('10.128.0.0/20'). A client connecting via Private Service Connect from a GCP VPC with IP 10.200.1.5 (outside the allowed range) attempts to connect. What is the expected behavior?

options.text: Connection is blocked because 10.200.1.5 is not in the ALLOWED_IP_LIST
Connection fails, and the user must use a VPN to route traffic through the allowed IP range
Connection succeeds because Private Service Connect traffic bypasses IP-based network policy enforcement
Connection is blocked until the administrator explicitly enables Private Service Connect in the network policy

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. GCP Private Service Connect traffic arrives at Snowflake through GCP's private network backbone without presenting a standard public IP for IP-based network policy evaluation. Standard IP-based network policies are bypassed for Private Service Connect connections. To enforce access controls for these connections, administrators should use VPC-based network rules.

**A** is incorrect. The connection does not behave like a standard internet connection where the source IP is checked against the ALLOWED_IP_LIST — Private Service Connect bypasses IP-based network policy enforcement.

**B** is incorrect. Using a VPN to route through an allowed IP range would work but misses the key insight: Private Service Connect connections are already exempt from IP policy enforcement.
