**D** is incorrect. There is no explicit Private Service Connect enablement required in a network policy — the bypass behavior is automatic for private connectivity traffic.

[Network Policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A security team wants to restrict Snowflake inbound connections to only specific AWS VPC endpoints. They plan to use network rules. Select TWO accurate statements about creating an AWSVPCEID network rule. (Select TWO)

options.text: The TYPE = AWSVPCEID network rule accepts VPC endpoint IDs (e.g., vpce-0123456789abcdef0) in the VALUE_LIST
AWSVPCEID rules are only available on Business Critical edition and above
An AWSVPCEID network rule must be included in a network policy to take effect
AWSVPCEID rules can be combined with TYPE = IPV4 rules in the same network policy to allow both VPC endpoint and IP-based access

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. TYPE = AWSVPCEID network rules accept VPC endpoint IDs in the vpce-xxxxxxxx format in the VALUE_LIST.

**B** is incorrect. AWSVPCEID rules are not limited to Business Critical edition — they are available on standard Snowflake accounts with PrivateLink-capable deployments.

**C** is correct. Like all network rules, AWSVPCEID rules must be added to a network policy (included in the network rule list) for the restriction to be enforced.

**D** is incorrect as stated. While different network rule types can conceptually be combined, combining AWSVPCEID and IPV4 rules within the same network policy does require specific policy structure — the option as phrased overstates the simplicity without qualification.

[Network Policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A Snowflake table holds reference data that never changes. Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 on this table would save storage costs. What is the trade-off the administrator must accept?

options.text: The table cannot be queried after retention is set to 0
Time Travel is disabled; if data is accidentally modified or deleted, it cannot be recovered using Time Travel or AT/BEFORE queries
Setting retention to 0 also disables Fail-Safe, so the table has no recovery options whatsoever
The table is automatically converted to a TRANSIENT table type when retention is set to 0

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 disables Time Travel for that table. AT/BEFORE queries cannot reference historical states and accidental DML changes or drops cannot be recovered using Time Travel.

**A** is incorrect. The table remains fully queryable for current data — only the ability to query historical states is disabled.

**C** is incorrect. For permanent tables with retention 0, Fail-Safe still applies (7-day Snowflake-managed protection). Fail-Safe is separate from Time Travel and cannot be disabled by the customer on permanent tables.

**D** is incorrect. Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 does not convert a permanent table to a TRANSIENT table — the table type remains unchanged.

[Fail-Safe](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe)

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer needs a table that persists data between ETL pipeline stages (visible to multiple sessions and roles) but should NOT accumulate Fail-Safe storage costs since the data is easily regeneratable. Which table type is most appropriate?

options.text: PERMANENT table with DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0
TEMPORARY table with shared visibility across sessions
TRANSIENT table
EXTERNAL table pointing to an S3 stage

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. A TRANSIENT table persists across sessions and is visible to all roles (unlike temporary tables), but has no Fail-Safe period (unlike permanent tables). Transient tables support 0 or 1 day of Time Travel only, making them cost-efficient for intermediate ETL data that is regeneratable.

**A** is incorrect. A permanent table with DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 still has a 7-day Fail-Safe period — it would continue to accumulate the Fail-Safe storage costs the developer wants to avoid.

**B** is incorrect. TEMPORARY tables are session-scoped — they are not visible to other sessions or roles, which contradicts the requirement for shared visibility across sessions.

**D** is incorrect. EXTERNAL tables are read-only and backed by external cloud storage — they cannot be written to by the ETL pipeline as an intermediate staging table.

[Fail-Safe](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe)

questions.stem: After running a large bulk insert operation, an administrator wants to check whether the DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on the target table matches the schema-level setting. Which command shows this information for all tables in a schema?

options.text: SELECT TABLE_NAME, RETENTION_TIME FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'MY_SCHEMA';
DESCRIBE DATABASE mydb;
SHOW SCHEMAS IN DATABASE mydb;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'MY_SCHEMA';

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES includes a RETENTION_TIME column that shows the effective DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS for each table. Filtering by TABLE_SCHEMA allows checking all tables in a specific schema and comparing table-level retention against the schema default.

**B** is incorrect. DESCRIBE DATABASE shows database-level metadata (like the database owner and comment) — not individual table retention settings.

**C** is incorrect. SHOW SCHEMAS IN DATABASE shows schema properties including the schema-level retention setting — not the individual table retention values.

**D** is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS shows storage volume per table (ACTIVE_BYTES, TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES, FAILSAFE_BYTES, etc.) — not the retention time configuration setting.

[INFORMATION_SCHEMA TABLES View](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/info-schema/tables)

questions.stem: A Snowflake warehouse is currently configured as STANDARD and the team wants to migrate memory-intensive Snowpark workloads to it without changing the existing warehouse name (other tools use the name). Which command changes the warehouse type in place?

options.text: ALTER WAREHOUSE my_wh SET MEMORY_PER_CLUSTER_BYTES = MAXIMUM;
ALTER WAREHOUSE my_wh MODIFY TYPE SNOWPARK_OPTIMIZED;
GRANT SNOWPARK_OPTIMIZED_MEMORY ON WAREHOUSE my_wh TO ROLE sysadmin;
ALTER WAREHOUSE my_wh SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED;

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. ALTER WAREHOUSE warehouse_name SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED converts an existing warehouse to the Snowpark-Optimized type in place without recreating it. This preserves the warehouse name and existing parameter configurations.

**B** is incorrect. MODIFY TYPE is not valid Snowflake ALTER WAREHOUSE syntax — the correct clause is SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE.

**C** is incorrect. GRANT SNOWPARK_OPTIMIZED_MEMORY is not a valid Snowflake privilege or command — warehouse type is changed via ALTER WAREHOUSE, not a privilege grant.

**A** is incorrect. SET MEMORY_PER_CLUSTER_BYTES is not a valid warehouse parameter name in Snowflake.

[Snowpark-Optimized Warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-snowpark-optimized)

questions.stem: An administrator queries ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS and finds TABLE_X with FAILSAFE_BYTES = 0 despite the account being on Enterprise edition. What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: TABLE_X has never had any data — it is empty
The table owner disabled Fail-Safe using ALTER TABLE TABLE_X SET FAILSAFE = DISABLED
FAILSAFE_BYTES accumulates slowly and has not had enough time to build up
TABLE_X is a TRANSIENT or TEMPORARY table, which have no Fail-Safe period

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Transient and temporary tables have no Fail-Safe period in Snowflake by design. If TABLE_X is a TRANSIENT or TEMPORARY table, FAILSAFE_BYTES will always be 0 regardless of the Snowflake edition.

**A** is incorrect. An empty permanent table would also show FAILSAFE_BYTES = 0, but the most likely explanation for a table on Enterprise with no Fail-Safe is that it is a transient or temporary table — not simply an empty permanent table.

**C** is incorrect. Fail-Safe does not accumulate slowly — FAILSAFE_BYTES reflects the amount of data currently within the 7-day Fail-Safe window. If there is data, FAILSAFE_BYTES would show it.

**B** is incorrect. Customers cannot disable Fail-Safe using ALTER TABLE — Fail-Safe on permanent tables is a Snowflake-managed feature that cannot be turned off by users.

[Fail-Safe](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe)

questions.stem: A financial company needs to track all data transferred out of their Snowflake environment for GDPR egress compliance reporting. Specifically they need to know the volume of data (bytes) transferred to each destination region. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view satisfies this requirement?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY WHERE QUERY_TYPE = 'COPY'
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.COPY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY records all data transfer events including BYTES_TRANSFERRED, SOURCE_CLOUD, SOURCE_REGION, TARGET_CLOUD, TARGET_REGION, and TRANSFER_TYPE. This view is specifically designed for egress monitoring and compliance reporting.

**A** is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY captures query metadata and SQL text — it does not provide structured transfer volume data or destination region information.

**C** is incorrect. COPY_HISTORY tracks file-level COPY INTO operations, focusing on row counts and file load status — not egress volume by destination region.

**D** is incorrect. PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY tracks Snowpipe ingestion (inbound data loading credits) — not egress data transfer to external destinations.

[DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY View](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/data_transfer_history)

questions.stem: A security team wants to identify all Snowflake sessions opened using outdated Python connector versions (below 3.0.0) to enforce connector upgrade policies. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and column combination allows this filtering?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY — CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID column
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS — CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID column containing 'PythonConnector' and version
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — CLIENT_DRIVER column
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS — CONNECTOR_VERSION column

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS contains the CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID column which records the client application name and version for each session (e.g., 'PythonConnector 2.9.0'). By filtering WHERE CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID LIKE 'PythonConnector%' and extracting the version number, administrators can identify outdated connector sessions.

**A** is incorrect. LOGIN_HISTORY has CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID for login events but sessions created after the initial login event may not all be captured there — SESSIONS is the more complete view for ongoing session analysis.

**C** is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY has per-query client info but requires deduplication to identify unique sessions, and not every session produces queries that would appear in query history.

**D** is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS does not have a CONNECTOR_VERSION column — user records contain profile information, not per-session connector version data.

[SESSIONS View](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/sessions)

questions.stem: An ORGADMIN needs to identify which accounts in the Snowflake organization have exceeded 10,000 credits consumed this month. Where can the ORGADMIN find cross-account credit consumption data?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY — query this from each account separately and aggregate
SNOWFLAKE.ORGANIZATION_USAGE.USAGE_IN_CURRENCY_DAILY — shows credit consumption converted to currency, accessed via the ORGADMIN role
SNOWFLAKE.ORGANIZATION_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY — provides daily credit totals per account across the organization
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.RESOURCE_MONITORS — shows cumulative credit consumption for each account in the org

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. ORGANIZATION_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY (accessible to ORGADMIN) provides daily credit totals per account across the entire organization, enabling identification of accounts that exceeded a credit threshold.

**A** is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY is scoped to a single account — it cannot aggregate credit consumption across multiple accounts from a single query.

**B** is incorrect for this specific requirement. USAGE_IN_CURRENCY_DAILY converts credit consumption to currency amounts — useful for cost reporting, but the question asks about credit counts (10,000 credits), making the raw credit view more directly applicable.

**D** is incorrect. SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.RESOURCE_MONITORS is not a valid account usage view, and resource monitors are account-level constructs that do not provide cross-account organization-level reporting.
